 {"id":"ntm-bke.1","title":"Project Config Discovery (.ntm/)","description":"Auto-detect and load .ntm/ directory configuration from project roots.

## Overview
When running NTM commands in a project directory, automatically discover and load .ntm/config.toml to customize behavior per-project.

## Directory Structure
```
myproject/
├── .ntm/
│   ├── config.toml      # Project settings
│   ├── palette.md       # Project commands
│   └── templates/       # Project templates
├── src/
└── ...
```

## Discovery Algorithm
```go
func FindProjectConfig(startDir string) (*ProjectConfig, error) {
    dir := startDir
    for {
        configPath := filepath.Join(dir, ".ntm", "config.toml")
        if fileExists(configPath) {
            return LoadProjectConfig(configPath)
        }
        
        parent := filepath.Dir(dir)
        if parent == dir { // Reached root
            return nil, nil // No project config
        }
        dir = parent
    }
}
```

## Config Merge Order
1. Hardcoded defaults (in binary)
2. Global config (~/.config/ntm/config.toml)
3. Project config (.ntm/config.toml)
4. CLI flags

Later sources override earlier ones.

```go
func LoadMergedConfig() *Config {
    cfg := DefaultConfig()
    
    if global, err := LoadGlobalConfig(); err == nil {
        cfg = MergeConfig(cfg, global)
    }
    
    if project, err := FindProjectConfig(cwd); err == nil && project != nil {
        cfg = MergeConfig(cfg, project)
    }
    
    return cfg
}
```

## Project Config Schema
```toml
# .ntm/config.toml

# Default agent counts when spawning
[defaults]
agents = { cc = 2, cod = 1 }  # No Gemini for this project

# Project palette extends global
[palette]
file = "palette.md"  # Relative to .ntm/

# Project templates extend global
[templates]
dir = "templates"  # Relative to .ntm/

# Custom agent commands for this project
[agents]
# Override default Claude command
claude = "claude --project /path/to/claude-config"
```

## Init Command
```bash
ntm config project init           # Create .ntm/ scaffold
ntm config project init --force   # Overwrite existing
```

Creates:
```
.ntm/
├── config.toml    # With comments explaining options
├── palette.md     # Empty palette scaffold
└── templates/     # Empty directory
```

## Detection in Commands
All commands that use config should call merged config:
```go
func newSpawnCmd() *cobra.Command {
    return &cobra.Command{
        RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
            cfg := config.LoadMerged() // <-- Uses project config if present
            // ...
        },
    }
}
```

## Files to Modify/Create
- internal/config/project.go - Project config discovery
- internal/config/merge.go - Config merging logic
- internal/cli/config.go - Add 'project init' subcommand","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:43:23.935269-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T17:03:08.937094-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-bke.1","depends_on_id":"ntm-bke","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:43:23.936439-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

-{"id":"ntm-c2b","title":"EPIC: Plugin & Extension System","description":"Allow users to extend NTM with custom agents, commands, and hooks.

## Problem Statement

NTM currently supports three hardcoded agent types: Claude, Codex, Gemini.
Users may want to use other agents or customize behavior:

1. **Other AI Agents**: Cursor, Aider, Continue, OpenAI API, local LLMs
2. **Custom Workflows**: Project-specific commands and automation
3. **Integration Hooks**: Run scripts before/after NTM operations
4. **Team Customization**: Share agent configs across a team

Currently, users must fork NTM to add new agent types or modify behavior.

## Goals

1. Allow custom agent definitions via config files
2. Support custom commands as executable scripts
3. Provide hook system for pre/post command execution
4. Make plugins discoverable and easy to install
5. Document plugin development clearly

## Why This Matters

Extensibility enables:
- Immediate support for any new AI coding agent
- Project-specific workflows without code changes
- Team-shared configurations and standards
- Community plugin ecosystem
- Future-proofing against agent landscape changes

## Success Criteria

- Users can define custom agents in ~/.config/ntm/agents/
- ntm spawn myproject --cursor=2 (custom agent)
- Users can add commands via ~/.config/ntm/commands/
- ntm my-custom-command (runs script)
- Hooks fire at defined extension points
- ntm plugins list shows installed plugins

## Plugin Types

### 1. Agent Plugins (~/.config/ntm/agents/*.toml)

Define new agent types:

# cursor.toml
[agent]
name = "cursor"
alias = "cur"
command = "cursor --headless --ai-mode"
description = "Cursor AI editor"
default_tags = ["cursor", "editor"]

Usage: ntm spawn myproject --cursor=2

### 2. Command Plugins (~/.config/ntm/commands/*)

Executable scripts become commands:

#\!/bin/bash
# ~/.config/ntm/commands/sync-all
# Description: Sync all agents with latest code

for session in $(ntm list --json | jq -r '.[].name'); do
  ntm send "$session" --all "git pull && review changes"
done

Usage: ntm sync-all

### 3. Hook Plugins (~/.config/ntm/hooks/*)

Run before/after NTM commands:

# hooks.toml
[hooks.pre_send]
command = "~/.config/ntm/hooks/log-prompt.sh"
pass_args = true

[hooks.post_spawn]
command = "notify-send 'NTM' 'Session created'"

## Technical Approach

Plugin Discovery:
- Scan known directories on startup
- Cache plugin metadata for performance
- Reload on ntm plugins refresh

Agent Plugin Loading:
- Parse TOML to AgentConfig struct
- Register with spawn/add commands
- Generate shell integration aliases

Command Plugin Execution:
- Discover executables in commands/
- Run with NTM context in environment
- Pass JSON via stdin, capture stdout/stderr

Hook Execution:
- Define extension points in each command
- Run hooks synchronously (pre) or async (post)
- Pass command context as JSON
- Fail-safe: hook errors don't break NTM

## Security Considerations

- Plugins are user-installed, user-trusted
- No automatic plugin download/installation
- Clear documentation of execution context
- Hooks can see all command arguments
- Consider --no-hooks flag for sensitive operations

## Interaction with Other Features

- JSON Output: Plugin commands can use --json
- Config: Plugins extend config schema
- Remote: Plugins work locally, commands sent to remote
- Tags: Custom agents can have default tags

## Future Possibilities

- Plugin registry/marketplace
- Version pinning for plugins
- Plugin dependencies
- Programmatic plugin API (Go plugins)

## Problem Statement

NTM currently supports three hardcoded agent types: Claude, Codex, Gemini.
Users may want to use other agents or customize behavior:

4. **Team Customization**: Share agent configs across a team

Currently, users must fork NTM to add new agent types or modify behavior.

## Goals

1. Allow custom agent definitions via config files
2. Support custom commands as executable scripts
3. Provide hook system for pre/post command execution
4. Make plugins discoverable and easy to install
5. Document plugin development clearly

## Why This Matters

Extensibility enables:
- Immediate support for any new AI coding agent
- Project-specific workflows without code changes

### 1. Agent Plugins (~/.config/ntm/agents/*.toml)

# cursor.toml
[agent]
name = "cursor"

command = "cursor --headless --ai-mode"

### 2. Command Plugins (~/.config/ntm/commands/*)

#\!/bin/bash
# ~/.config/ntm/commands/sync-all

### 3. Hook Plugins (~/.config/ntm/hooks/*)

# hooks.toml

 {"id":"ntm-c36","title":"Implement --robot-health for agent health matrix","description":"Focused health check returning responsiveness and activity metrics.

## Rationale
--robot-snapshot includes health data, but sometimes I just need a quick health check without the full state. This is the lightweight "are things working" query.

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "checked_at": "ISO8601",
  "system": {
    "tmux_ok": true,
    "disk_free_gb": 42.5,
    "load_avg": 1.2
  },
  "sessions": {
    "myproject": {
      "healthy": true,
      "agents": {
        "0.1": {"responsive": true, "output_rate": "high", "last_activity_sec": 2},
        "0.2": {"responsive": true, "output_rate": "low", "last_activity_sec": 45},
        "0.3": {"responsive": false, "issue": "no_output_5min"}
      }
    }
  },
  "alerts": ["0.3: no output 5min"]
}
```

## Health Heuristics
- responsive: has pane produced output recently AND is tmux pane alive
- output_rate: high (>1 line/sec), medium, low (<1 line/min), none
- last_activity_sec: time since last output line

## Alert Conditions
- No output for 5 minutes (configurable)
- Pane process exited
- High CPU in pane (if detectable)
- Disk space low
- Session unexpectedly killed

## Use Cases
1. Quick sanity check before sending batch instructions
2. Detect stuck agents for restart
3. System health monitoring
4. Pre-flight check before spawning more agents","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"BlackSnow","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:51:22.401882-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T09:26:09.134385-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T09:26:09.134385-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-ceb","title":"Create internal/agentmail HTTP client package","description":"# Create internal/agentmail HTTP Client Package

## Purpose

Foundation for all Agent Mail integration. This package provides a clean Go interface to the MCP Agent Mail HTTP API.

## Technical Details

### Package Structure
```
internal/agentmail/
├── client.go       # HTTP client with connection pooling, retries
├── types.go        # Go structs matching Agent Mail data model
├── tools.go        # Methods for each MCP tool (send_message, fetch_inbox, etc.)
├── resources.go    # Methods for MCP resources (resource://inbox, etc.)
├── errors.go       # Error types with Agent Mail error codes
└── client_test.go  # Unit tests with mock server
```

### Client Configuration
```go
type Client struct {
    baseURL     string       // Default: http://127.0.0.1:8765/mcp/
    bearerToken string       // From AGENT_MAIL_TOKEN or config
    httpClient  *http.Client // With timeouts, connection pooling
    projectKey  string       // Cached project path
}

func NewClient(opts ...Option) (*Client, error)
func (c *Client) IsAvailable() bool  // Health check
```

### Key Types (matching Agent Mail schema)
```go
type Agent struct {
    ID              int       `json:"id"`
    Name            string    `json:"name"`            // e.g., "GreenCastle"
    Program         string    `json:"program"`         // e.g., "claude-code"
    Model           string    `json:"model"`           // e.g., "opus-4.1"
    TaskDescription string    `json:"task_description"`
    LastActiveTS    time.Time `json:"last_active_ts"`
}

type Message struct {
    ID          int       `json:"id"`
    ThreadID    string    `json:"thread_id"`
    Subject     string    `json:"subject"`
    BodyMD      string    `json:"body_md"`
    From        string    `json:"from"`
    To          []string  `json:"to"`
    Importance  string    `json:"importance"`  // normal, high, urgent
    AckRequired bool      `json:"ack_required"`
    CreatedTS   time.Time `json:"created_ts"`
}

type FileReservation struct {
    ID          int       `json:"id"`
    PathPattern string    `json:"path_pattern"`
    AgentName   string    `json:"agent_name"`
    Exclusive   bool      `json:"exclusive"`
    Reason      string    `json:"reason"`
    ExpiresTS   time.Time `json:"expires_ts"`
}
```

### JSON-RPC 2.0 Protocol
Agent Mail uses JSON-RPC 2.0 over HTTP:
```go
type JSONRPCRequest struct {
    JSONRPC string      `json:"jsonrpc"` // "2.0"
    ID      interface{} `json:"id"`
    Method  string      `json:"method"`  // "tools/call"
    Params  struct {
        Name      string                 `json:"name"`
        Arguments map[string]interface{} `json:"arguments"`
    } `json:"params"`
}

type JSONRPCResponse struct {
    JSONRPC string          `json:"jsonrpc"`
    ID      interface{}     `json:"id"`
    Result  json.RawMessage `json:"result,omitempty"`
    Error   *JSONRPCError   `json:"error,omitempty"`
}
```

## Implementation Notes

- **Connection pooling**: Reuse HTTP connections; Agent Mail may be called frequently
- **Timeout handling**: 5s default for most operations, 30s for search/summarize
- **Retry logic**: Exponential backoff for transient failures (503, timeouts)
- **Bearer token**: Read from NTM config or AGENT_MAIL_TOKEN env var
- **Graceful degradation**: All methods return (result, error); callers handle missing server

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Client can connect to Agent Mail and verify health
- [ ] All MCP tools mapped to Go methods with proper types
- [ ] Error handling covers all Agent Mail error codes
- [ ] Unit tests with mock HTTP server
- [ ] Connection pooling verified with concurrent requests
- [ ] Timeout and retry behavior documented and tested","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:17:29.953845-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:20.670088-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:20.670088-05:00"}
@@ -182,6 +182,7 @@
 {"id":"ntm-je6","title":"EPIC: Git Coordination Layer","description":"# Git Coordination Layer

## Background & Motivation

When multiple agents edit the same codebase concurrently, git conflicts are inevitable:
- Agent A modifies src/api/users.py
- Agent B simultaneously modifies the same file
- One commit overwrites the other, or merge conflicts arise

## Integration Levels

This EPIC has TWO levels:

### Level 1: Basic Git Visibility (No Agent Mail Required)
- `ntm git status`: Show git state per agent pane
- `ntm git sync`: Pull/push across panes
- Works without Agent Mail

### Level 2: Coordinated Locking (Requires Agent Mail)
- `ntm lock`: Reserve files via Agent Mail
- `ntm unlock`: Release reservations
- Conflict detection before spawn
- Pre-commit guard integration

## Commands

### ntm git status (Level 1)
```bash
ntm git status myproject
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Git Status: myproject                                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Branch: feature/auth                                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Pane      │ Modified │ Staged │ Status                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ cc_1      │ 3 files  │ 1 file │ uncommitted changes       │
│ cc_2      │ 0 files  │ 0 files│ clean                      │
│ cod_1     │ 2 files  │ 0 files│ uncommitted changes       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### ntm git sync (Level 1)
```bash
ntm git sync myproject
# Pulls latest in all panes (with user confirmation)
# Shows conflicts if any

ntm git sync myproject --check
# Preview what would happen, don't execute
```

### ntm lock / ntm unlock (Level 2 - Requires Agent Mail)
```bash
ntm lock myproject "src/api/**" --reason "Refactoring"
ntm unlock myproject --all
ntm locks myproject  # Show all reservations
```

If Agent Mail unavailable:
```
⚠️ File locking requires Agent Mail (not detected)
  Start Agent Mail: mcp-agent-mail serve
  Or continue without locking (not recommended for multi-agent work)
```

## Graceful Degradation

| Feature | Agent Mail Available | Agent Mail Unavailable |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| ntm git status | Full (with locks) | Basic (git only) |
| ntm git sync | Full | Full |
| ntm lock/unlock | Works | Error with help |
| Conflict prevention | Works | Not available |

## Success Criteria

- [ ] ntm git status shows per-pane git state
- [ ] ntm git sync pulls across panes safely
- [ ] Level 2 features require Agent Mail (with helpful errors)
- [ ] Graceful degradation documented
- [ ] Pre-commit guard optional","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:27:30.523699-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:41:49.038113-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-je6","depends_on_id":"ntm-vum","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:29:38.953187-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-k7o","title":"Integrate --ssh flag with all session commands","description":"Add --ssh support to spawn, list, attach, send, status, kill.

## What This Task Does

After the SSH wrapper (ntm-5zs) is complete, integrate --ssh flag
with all commands that operate on sessions.

## Commands to Update

- ntm spawn --ssh host: Create session on remote
- ntm list --ssh host: List remote sessions
- ntm attach --ssh host: Attach to remote session
- ntm send --ssh host: Send prompt to remote agents
- ntm status --ssh host: Get remote session status
- ntm kill --ssh host: Kill remote session

## Implementation Pattern

For each command:
1. Add --ssh flag (string, optional)
2. Pass to TmuxClient constructor
3. All tmux operations use client abstraction

func runSpawn(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
    sshHost, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("ssh")
    client := tmux.NewClient(sshHost)
    
    // All operations use client
    if err := client.CreateSession(session); err != nil {
        return err
    }
    ...
}

## Session Host Persistence

When spawning remotely, store host in tmux environment:
client.SetEnvironment(session, "NTM_REMOTE_HOST", sshHost)

When operating on existing session without --ssh:
1. Get session's NTM_REMOTE_HOST
2. If set, use it
3. If not, assume local

## Error Handling

- Connection timeout: Clear message, suggest checking connectivity
- Auth failure: Suggest ssh-agent or key path
- Remote tmux not found: Suggest installing tmux on remote

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] --ssh works on spawn
- [ ] --ssh works on list  
- [ ] --ssh works on attach
- [ ] --ssh works on send
- [ ] --ssh works on status
- [ ] --ssh works on kill
- [ ] Remote host auto-detected for existing sessions

 {"id":"ntm-sim","title":"Implement --robot-snapshot for unified state query","description":"Core orchestration primitive: single command returns complete system state including all sessions, agent health, bead summary, mail status, and alerts. This is THE most important flag because it eliminates the need for multiple queries.

## Rationale
When orchestrating N agents across M sessions working on P beads with Q mail threads, I currently need separate queries for each dimension. This compounds latency (4-6 tool calls) and burns tokens on redundant parsing. A unified snapshot gives me complete situational awareness in one atomic read.

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "ts": "ISO8601",
  "sessions": [{
    "name": "string",
    "agents": [{
      "pane": "window.pane",
      "type": "claude|codex|gemini|cursor|windsurf|aider",
      "state": "active|idle|stuck|waiting",
      "last_output_age_sec": number,
      "output_tail_lines": number,
      "current_bead": "bead-id|null",
      "pending_mail": number
    }]
  }],
  "beads_summary": {"open": N, "in_progress": N, "blocked": N, "ready": N},
  "mail_unread": number,
  "alerts": ["string"]
}
```

## Key Design Decisions
- Always include everything (no optional fields that require follow-up)
- Use compact field names (abbreviations OK for frequently-accessed data)
- Include computed fields (last_output_age_sec vs raw timestamp)
- Alerts are pre-filtered to actionable items only

## Integration Points
- tmux: session/pane enumeration, output capture
- beads: bd list --json integration  
- agent-mail: inbox count per agent
- pane title parsing for agent type detection","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:50:07.458116-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:20:34.255328-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T03:20:34.255328-05:00","comments":[{"id":10,"issue_id":"ntm-sim","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Implementation Architecture

### File Structure
```
internal/robot/
├── robot.go          # Existing - add Snapshot type and function
├── snapshot.go       # NEW - SnapshotOutput, BuildSnapshot()
├── integrations.go   # NEW - BeadsIntegration, MailIntegration
└── alerts.go         # NEW - AlertManager, Alert types
```

### Core Type
```go
type SnapshotOutput struct {
    Timestamp   time.Time              `json:"ts"`
    Sessions    []SessionSnapshot      `json:"sessions"`
    Beads       *BeadsSnapshot         `json:"beads,omitempty"`
    AgentMail   *MailSnapshot          `json:"agent_mail,omitempty"`
    Alerts      []Alert                `json:"alerts"`
    Summary     SnapshotSummary        `json:"summary"`
}
```

### Flag Registration
In root.go init():
```go
rootCmd.Flags().Bool("robot-snapshot", false, "Output complete system state as JSON")
rootCmd.Flags().String("since", "", "Return only changes since timestamp (ISO8601)")
```

### Performance Target
- Cold snapshot: <100ms
- With caching: <20ms
- Memory: <5MB for 10 sessions with 50 panes","created_at":"2025-12-10T07:55:34Z"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-t0ch","title":"Implement file conflict detection","description":"# Implement File Conflict Detection

## Purpose

Detect when multiple agents modify the same file, potentially creating conflicts.

## Detection Logic

```go
type Conflict struct {
    Path   string
    Agents []AgentChange
}

func DetectConflicts(changes []AgentChange) []Conflict {
    byPath := make(map[string][]AgentChange)
    for _, c := range changes {
        byPath[c.File] = append(byPath[c.File], c)
    }
    
    var conflicts []Conflict
    for path, agents := range byPath {
        if len(agents) > 1 {
            conflicts = append(conflicts, Conflict{
                Path:   path,
                Agents: agents,
            })
        }
    }
    return conflicts
}
```

## Severity Levels

- **Warning**: Same file modified by 2+ agents (may or may not conflict)
- **Critical**: Same lines modified (if we can detect via git diff)

## Integration with ntm send --all

After broadcast completes:
```
ntm send myproject --all "Implement the feature"
# ... agents work ...
# On completion:
⚠️ Potential conflict: src/api/users.py modified by cc_1, cc_2
  Run `ntm conflicts myproject` for details
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Detect same-file modifications across agents
- [ ] Warn user after broadcasts
- [ ] ntm conflicts command shows details
- [ ] Severity classification (warning vs critical)","notes":"Picking up highest-priority free task; conflict detection","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:29:14.387332-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T20:43:54.633041-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T20:43:54.633041-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-t0ch","depends_on_id":"ntm-xkvu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:29:37.60962-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"ntm-t7ol","title":"Phase 3: Auto-Trigger on Rate Limit Detection","description":"# Phase 3: Auto-Trigger on Rate Limit Detection

## What This Is
Automatically trigger rotation assistance when a rate limit is detected, instead of requiring user to manually run `ntm rotate`.

## Leverages Existing Infrastructure
NTM already has rate limit detection in `internal/health/health.go`:
```go
var errorPatterns = []struct {
    Pattern *regexp.Regexp
    Type    string
    Message string
}{
    {regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)rate.?limit`), "rate_limit", "Rate limit detected"},
    {regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)429`), "rate_limit", "HTTP 429 rate limit"},
    {regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)too.?many.?requests`), "rate_limit", "Too many requests"},
    {regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)quota.?exceeded`), "rate_limit", "Quota exceeded"},
}
```

And notification in `internal/notify/notify.go`:
```go
EventRateLimit EventType = "agent.rate_limit"
```

We just need to **connect** limit detection → rotation trigger.

## Implementation Approach

### Option A: Background Monitor (Recommended)
Add rotation trigger to the resilience monitor:

```go
// In internal/resilience/monitor.go

func (m *Monitor) handleRateLimit(agent *AgentState, waitSeconds int) {
    // Existing notification
    if m.cfg.RateLimit.Notify {
        m.notifier.Notify(notify.NewRateLimitEvent(...))
    }
    
    // NEW: Trigger rotation assistance
    if m.cfg.Rotation.AutoTrigger {
        m.triggerRotationAssistance(agent)
    }
}

func (m *Monitor) triggerRotationAssistance(agent *AgentState) {
    // Send desktop notification with action hint
    m.notifier.Notify(notify.Event{
        Type:    notify.EventRotationNeeded,
        Message: fmt.Sprintf("Rate limit hit! Run: ntm rotate %s --pane=%d", m.session, agent.PaneIndex),
    })
    
    // Optionally auto-initiate rotation (if configured)
    if m.cfg.Rotation.AutoInitiate {
        // Start the rotation flow in a separate goroutine
        go m.initiateRotation(agent)
    }
}
```

### Option B: Dashboard Integration
When dashboard is open and detects limit, show prominent alert:

```
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  ⚠️  RATE LIMIT DETECTED on pane 0 (Claude)                   ║
║                                                               ║
║  Press 'r' to rotate account, or run: ntm rotate --pane=0     ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

### Option C: tmux Alert (Simplest)
When running `ntm spawn` with monitoring, show tmux message:

```go
func alertRateLimit(session string, paneIdx int) {
    msg := fmt.Sprintf("Rate limit! Run: ntm rotate %s --pane=%d", session, paneIdx)
    exec.Command("tmux", "display-message", "-d", "10000", msg).Run()
}
```

## Configuration
```toml
[rotation]
# Existing fields...
auto_trigger = true       # Show notification when limit detected
auto_initiate = false     # Actually start rotation automatically (aggressive)
```

## What This Builds On
- Phase 1: Core rotation command
- Existing health detection
- Existing notification system

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Desktop notification when rate limit detected
- [ ] Notification includes command to run
- [ ] Dashboard shows alert (if open)
- [ ] Optional auto-initiation (disabled by default)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:14:52.846787-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T17:39:00.21314-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T17:39:00.21314-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-t7ol","depends_on_id":"ntm-cs4z","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:18:01.91758-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-vum","title":"EPIC: MCP Agent Mail Integration","description":"# MCP Agent Mail Integration

## Background & Motivation

NTM orchestrates multiple AI coding agents (Claude, Codex, Gemini) in parallel tmux panes. Currently, these agents operate in isolation - they can see the same filesystem but cannot:
- Communicate with each other directly
- Know what others are working on
- Coordinate file access to avoid conflicts
- Share status or hand off work

MCP Agent Mail provides exactly this coordination layer.

## CRITICAL CLARIFICATION: NTM's Role

**NTM is a COORDINATOR and BRIDGE, not an agent replacement.**

The agents running in panes (Claude Code, Codex, etc.) may or may not have their own Agent Mail integration:
- If they DO have Agent Mail support: NTM provides unified visibility (aggregate inbox, lock status)
- If they DON'T: NTM acts as coordinator, sending messages on behalf of the user

**Key insight**: NTM doesn't pretend to BE the agents. It's the orchestration layer that:
1. Provides a unified view of all agent mail activity
2. Allows the USER to send messages to/between agents
3. Shows file reservation status across all agents
4. Detects conflicts before spawning new agents

## What NTM's Agent Mail Integration Provides

### 1. Visibility Layer
- Aggregate inbox view: See messages across all registered agents
- File lock status: Who has reserved what files
- Agent directory: Which agents are active in the project

### 2. User-to-Agent Communication
```bash
ntm mail send myproject --to cc_1 "Please focus on the API endpoint"
```
This sends FROM the NTM coordinator TO the specified agent. It's the user speaking through NTM.

### 3. Session Coordination
```bash
ntm lock myproject "src/api/**" --reason "User-directed refactor"
```
NTM can reserve files on behalf of the session, signaling to all agents that these files are being coordinated.

### 4. Conflict Prevention
```bash
ntm spawn myproject --cc=2
# Warning: src/api/** is reserved by GreenCastle (cc_1 in session 'other')
# Proceed? [y/N]
```

## Technical Approach

MCP Agent Mail runs as an HTTP server (default port 8765) with JSON-RPC 2.0.

NTM will:
1. **Create internal/agentmail package**: HTTP client for Agent Mail MCP tools
2. **Register as coordinator**: `ntm_<session>` identity for user-initiated actions
3. **Aggregate views**: Fetch inbox/locks from all known agents
4. **Expose commands**: ntm mail, ntm lock, ntm locks
5. **Dashboard integration**: Show mail and lock status
6. **Graceful degradation**: All features optional if Agent Mail unavailable

## Key Design Decisions

- **NTM is a coordinator**: Sends messages as `ntm_<session>`, not as individual agents
- **Agents own their mail**: If Claude Code has Agent Mail support, it handles its own inbox
- **NTM aggregates and visualizes**: Unified view across all project agents
- **Optional integration**: Works without Agent Mail; features degrade gracefully
- **No duplication**: Don't re-implement what agents already do

## What This Is NOT

- NOT a replacement for agent-side Agent Mail integration
- NOT pretending NTM "is" the Claude/Codex/Gemini agent
- NOT required for basic NTM functionality

## Success Criteria

- [ ] NTM can detect if Agent Mail server is running
- [ ] NTM registers coordinator identity per session
- [ ] Aggregate inbox shows messages across all project agents
- [ ] File reservation status visible in status/dashboard
- [ ] Conflicts detected before spawning in reserved areas
- [ ] Graceful degradation when Agent Mail unavailable","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"BrownMountain","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:17:05.78207-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T10:59:51.052088-05:00"}
 {"id":"ntm-vz9","title":"Implement ntm copy command with filtering","description":"Copy pane content to system clipboard with filtering options.

## What This Task Does

Create 'ntm copy' command to copy pane output to system clipboard.

## Interface

# Copy entire pane output
$ ntm copy myproject:1

# Copy last N lines
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --last 50

# Copy lines matching pattern
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --pattern 'ERROR.*'

# Copy only code blocks (markdown fences)
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --code

# Copy to file instead of clipboard
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --output result.txt

# Quiet mode (no confirmation message)
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --quiet

## Content Filtering

--last N: Capture last N lines of pane output
--pattern: Filter to lines matching regex
--code: Extract markdown code blocks only

Code block extraction:
```python
def hello():
    print('world')
```

Becomes:
def hello():
    print('world')

Multiple code blocks concatenated with newlines.

## Implementation

1. Capture pane content (existing CapturePaneOutput)
2. Apply filters in order:
   a. --last (if specified)
   b. --pattern (if specified)
   c. --code (if specified)
3. Copy to clipboard or file
4. Show confirmation unless --quiet

## JSON Output

$ ntm copy myproject:1 --json
{
  "source": "myproject:1",
  "lines": 42,
  "bytes": 1234,
  "destination": "clipboard"
}

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Basic copy to clipboard works
- [ ] --last N limits lines
- [ ] --pattern filters by regex
- [ ] --code extracts code blocks
- [ ] --output writes to file
- [ ] --quiet suppresses output
- [ ] --json shows copy metadata

func createTempConfig(t *testing.T, content string) string {
    f, _ := os.CreateTemp("", "ntm-config-*.toml")
    f.WriteString(content)
    f.Close()
    t.Cleanup(func() { os.Remove(f.Name()) })
    return f.Name()
}

 {"id":"ntm-ws2","title":"Create internal/output package with JSON/text formatters","description":"Create a unified output formatting system that all commands will use.

## What This Task Does

Create a new internal/output package that provides:
1. OutputFormat type (JSON, Text, Table)
2. Formatter interface that commands implement
3. Root-level --json flag handling
4. Consistent JSON schema patterns

## Why This Is First

This is the foundation for all JSON output. Every command will use this
package, so it needs to be designed well upfront.

## Technical Details

Package structure:
internal/output/
├── format.go      # OutputFormat enum, Formatter interface
├── json.go        # JSON-specific formatting helpers
├── text.go        # Text-specific formatting (existing patterns)
└── schema.go      # Common JSON schema types

Key types:
type OutputFormat int
const (
    FormatText OutputFormat = iota
    FormatJSON
)

type Formatter interface {
    Format(data interface{}, format OutputFormat, w io.Writer) error
}

Common JSON patterns:
- Timestamps as ISO 8601 strings
- Errors include 'error' field with message
- Lists are always arrays (even empty)
- Nested objects for complex data

## Implementation Steps

1. Create internal/output directory
2. Define OutputFormat enum and detection logic
3. Create Formatter interface
4. Add JSON helper functions (marshal with indent, error wrapping)
5. Add root --json flag to cobra root command
6. Create context passing for format flag

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] internal/output package exists with clean API
- [ ] --json flag added to root command
- [ ] Format detection works (flag, env var, pipe detection)
- [ ] JSON output is consistently formatted
- [ ] Package is documented with examples

 {"id":"ntm-x0h","title":"Unit Tests: internal/tmux package (5.4% → 80%)","description":"# Unit Tests: internal/tmux Package

## Current State
- Coverage: 5.4%
- Tests: 4 existing (session name validation, agent type detection)

## Test Approach
Use REAL tmux sessions. Each test creates a unique session and cleans up after.

## Test Cases

### session.go

#### Session Management
- [ ] TestCreateSession - creates real session, verifies it exists
- [ ] TestCreateSessionWithDir - verifies working directory is set
- [ ] TestCreateSessionDuplicate - error handling for existing session
- [ ] TestKillSession - kills session, verifies gone
- [ ] TestSessionExists - true/false for existing/non-existing

#### Pane Operations
- [ ] TestSplitWindow - creates new pane, returns correct ID
- [ ] TestSplitWindowMultiple - creates 5 panes, verifies indices
- [ ] TestSetPaneTitle - sets title, verifies with list-panes
- [ ] TestGetPanes - lists all panes with correct metadata
- [ ] TestCapturePaneOutput - captures recent output

#### Layout Operations
- [ ] TestApplyTiledLayout - tiles panes, verifies layout
- [ ] TestSelectPane - selects specific pane
- [ ] TestZoomPane - zooms and unzooms

#### Key Sending
- [ ] TestSendKeys - sends text, verifies in pane output
- [ ] TestSendKeysWithEnter - sends text with enter key

- [ ] TestAgentTypeFromTitle_EdgeCases - empty, special chars

#### Attachment
- [ ] TestAttachSession - attach to session (difficult to test)

### Helper Functions
- [ ] TestInTmux - detect if running in tmux
- [ ] TestIsInstalled - detect tmux installation

## Test Helpers Needed
```go
func createTestSession(t *testing.T) string {
    name := fmt.Sprintf("ntm_test_%d", time.Now().UnixNano())
    t.Cleanup(func() { KillSession(name) })
    CreateSession(name, os.TempDir())
    return name
}
```
